data_flow_trace.original: Governance calls setFee but never calls setExpiration; _expirationDurations[sourceType] stays 0
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: setExpiration still independent of setFee; default zero value is still possible at storage level (Solidity default).
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: No difference in storage default behavior.

data_flow_trace.original: Attacker calls OracleRequestQueue.request(sourceType, sourceId, data) with 1 ETH
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: request() entrypoint still exists with the same signature; anyone can call it.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: isSupported(sourceType, sourceId) must return true; msg.value >= _fees[sourceType]
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Same entrypoint, same validations prior to expiration computation.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Code now reads `uint64 duration = _expirationDurations[sourceType]; if (duration == 0) revert ExpirationNotConfigured(sourceType); uint64 expiresAt = uint64(block.timestamp) + duration;`. A zero duration triggers a revert before the request is stored.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Explicit zero-duration guard added (GCC-R2-005 fix in commit f9290d2). Previously no such check existed.

data_flow_trace.original: Request stored with expiresAt == block.timestamp (same block)
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Unreachable when duration==0; the revert at line 118 aborts the tx before storage writes. When duration>0 the minimum effective expiresAt is block.timestamp + 1.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Attack precondition (zero expiresAt offset) can no longer be produced through the normal governance misconfiguration path.

data_flow_trace.original: Attacker immediately calls refund(requestId) in same tx
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: refund() still callable by anyone; same-tx pattern unchanged.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Requires a valid requestId — but step 3 now blocks creation when duration==0.

data_flow_trace.original: Line 194 check is `if (block.timestamp < req.expiresAt)` — same-block means false, no revert
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The expiry check is still strict `<`, unchanged. If expiresAt == block.timestamp the refund would indeed be allowed in the same tx — but by step 3 fix this condition can no longer be produced through a zero-duration configuration.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Logic unchanged, but now unreachable via the documented attack vector because expiresAt = block.timestamp + duration with duration >= 1.

data_flow_trace.original: req.refunded=true; 1 ETH sent back to attacker
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Must pass the `< expiresAt` gate first.

data_flow_trace.original: Off-chain validators still deliver oracle data — free ride
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: RequestSubmitted event is only emitted if the request() call succeeds; with duration==0 the whole transaction reverts and no event is produced.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Event emission now gated by non-zero duration.

reasoning_chain: The issue relies on the invariant that _expirationDurations[sourceType] can remain 0 while _fees[sourceType] is configured, producing expiresAt == block.timestamp and enabling same-tx refund. In the current HEAD (a623eab), OracleRequestQueue.sol:117-118 explicitly reverts with ExpirationNotConfigured(sourceType) when duration == 0. This change is introduced by commit f9290d2 under audit ticket GCC-R2-005 ('fix(oracle): validate expiration duration is configured in OracleRequestQueue'). A regression test (test_Request_RevertWhenExpirationNotConfigured) exists at test/unit/oracle/OracleRequestQueue.t.sol:183-195. Therefore the attacker cannot create a request with zero expiration offset through the governance-misconfiguration path described in the issue; the free-oracle scenario is blocked at request submission time.

key_evidence.why_matters: Directly blocks the root cause — zero-duration default — preventing expiresAt from ever being set equal to block.timestamp via the default-storage path.

key_evidence.excerpt:
/// @notice Expiration duration not configured for source type
error ExpirationNotConfigured(uint32 sourceType);
key_evidence.why_matters: Dedicated error declared specifically for this misconfiguration fix.
Regression test pinning the fix; demonstrates that request() reverts when only fee is set without expiration.

counterargument: One could argue that governance could still call setExpiration(sourceType, 1) with a one-second duration, which would let an attacker wait one second and then refund in the next block. However, the original issue is specifically about the zero-default path where governance forgot to call setExpiration; with duration >= 1, the refund gate at line 211 requires block.timestamp >= expiresAt = requestedAt + duration, so a same-transaction free-oracle attack (the core exploit) is no longer feasible. A separate argument could be: the attack requires validators to fulfill the request before the revert is visible — but since the entire transaction including the RequestSubmitted event reverts atomically when duration==0, validators never observe the event. Thus neither counterargument preserves the exploit.
